summary: electron-orbit ships an import-time, env-keyed backdoor with hand-rolled TLS to an obfuscated host
details: On require()/import, dist/index.js runs setupAutoRenderOnImport at module top level, which calls prefetchRegisteredIcons in a Node context. That function is gated by a sha256 equality check against process.env.BuildType (hardcoded digest 0ceaa396e2134a9937563e841539f30a9995e6e8646e60dcec24b26dc5748295). When the env var matches the attacker-known plaintext, an XOR-obfuscated https URL is assembled and contacted. The network path does not use Node's built-in https/tls module; instead the package implements a full TLS 1.3 client (ECDHE P-256, HKDF, AES-128-GCM, ClientHello/ServerHello/Finished) on top of node:net, with the module name 'node:net', the 'connect' method, and the 'connect' event all XOR-obfuscated. Reimplementing TLS over a raw socket has no benign purpose for the package's stated 'icon prefetch' behavior; the design defeats endpoint TLS inspection, mTLS pinning, proxy enforcement, and HTTP-layer logging — characteristic of covert C2/exfiltration. The package also presents a cover narrative: install.js writes a fake 'native bootstrap' file with an ELF/Mach-O/PE magic prefix and a JSON blob (hostname, username, version) to bin/formatters/, plus manifest.json and install-log.json, but never executes it. package.json declares author 'OrbitDB Archive' and homepage github.com/orbitdb-archive/orbit-electron, while the published npm name electron-orbit is unrelated to the legitimate OrbitDB project — brand impersonation to raise install trust. Installer harm: any consumer that imports the package while running with the attacker-set BuildType env value triggers an outbound covert channel to an attacker-controlled host that bypasses standard TLS/proxy inspection, enabling command execution and/or data exfiltration.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
